title: What is an option, in plain English?

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: An option contract is best described as:
choices: An agreement that gives the right (not always the obligation) to buy or sell something at a set price before expiry
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Rights and deadlines — not forced ownership unless you choose certain strategies.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: Standard stock options in the U.S. usually control:
choices: 100 shares per contract (check your market — rules vary)
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Retail equity options are often quoted per share but traded in a standard size.

teachSlides.title: A ticket with an expiry date
teachSlides.body: An option is a contract tied to an underlying (like a stock). It has a strike price, an expiration date, and a premium you pay or collect. Think: you’re buying or selling a set of rules, not the stock itself — unless exercise happens.

teachSlides.title: Calls vs puts (preview)
teachSlides.body: Calls lean toward “right to buy.” Puts lean toward “right to sell.” Next lessons zoom in — for now, remember: same building blocks (underlying, strike, expiry, premium).

teachSlides.title: Risk is real
teachSlides.body: Options can expire worthless. Some strategies have defined risk; others (like selling naked calls) can be extremely dangerous. This app teaches concepts — not a recommendation to trade them.

recapSlide.title: Recap: the wrapper
recapSlide.body: Option = contract with underlying, strike, expiry, premium. US equity options are often 100 shares per contract. Calls and puts are different rights — we’ll separate them in the next cards.

masteryQuestions.prompt: The price you pay for the option contract is usually called:
masteryQuestions.explanation: Premium is the market price of the contract itself.

masteryQuestions.prompt: A listed equity option contract in the U.S. typically represents:
choices: 100 shares of the underlying (verify for your market)
masteryQuestions.explanation: US standard is 100 shares per contract; always confirm locally.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Buying an option to open often means:
choices: You pay premium and receive rights defined by the contract
You automatically own the stock forever
masteryQuestions.explanation: You pay for the contract; risk is often limited to premium for long options (plus fees).
